title: Neural Magic
subtitle: Deep-learning inference on CPUs (no GPU required)

steps.label: What They Do
steps.body:
Neural Magic's software accelerates deep learning inference on CPU-based servers — no GPU required. They use automated model sparsification (pruning + quantization) to compress models so inference runs efficiently on commodity CPUs.

Akamai and Neural Magic share MIT origins. Both HQ'd in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

steps.label: How They Run on Akamai
steps.body:
Neural Magic's DeepSparse engine runs on standard Linode CPU plans (Dedicated CPU). For workloads that fit a sparsified model, this is dramatically cheaper than GPU plans.

Fits Akamai's distributed inference story: small inference workloads at every PoP, on CPUs that are already there, instead of rare and expensive GPU capacity.

steps.label: When To Position
steps.body: Workloads where GPU cost is prohibitive: real-time fraud detection, recommendation scoring, document classification, edge anomaly detection. Especially compelling at the edge where GPUs are scarce but CPUs are everywhere.
"Neural Magic = AI inference on CPUs. Position when GPU cost or scarcity is the blocker."

quiz.question: A customer needs real-time fraud-scoring inference globally but can't justify GPU cost in 30 regions. Which QCP fits?

options.text: Neural Magic — sparsified models running on CPU Linodes everywhere
options.explain: CPUs are everywhere; GPUs are not. Sparsification makes CPU inference fast enough for real-time.
That's the cost problem they're trying to solve.

quiz.question: How does Neural Magic's approach align with Akamai's distributed-inference vision?

options.text: It centralises inference in one region
CPUs are widely available across 30+ Akamai regions; GPUs aren't. Sparsification puts inference everywhere CPUs already are
options.explain: Inference distribution requires hardware ubiquity. CPUs achieve that; GPUs don't.
Specifically the opposite — no new GPU hardware needed.

options.text: It only runs on edge devices
options.explain: Runs on Linode CPU plans — server-side, just on commodity hardware.
